comments.text: I just uploaded my paper Three-term arithmetic progressions of consecutive powerful numbers to the arXiv, exploring this question. For a quick overview, let $x_0, x_1, \ldots$ be the increasing sequence of positive integers $x$ for which $y \in \mathbb{N}$ exist that solve the equation $$x^2 - 2 = 7^3 y^2.$$By the theory of Pell equations this sequence of $x_0, x_1, \ldots$ is infinite and we have $x_k = \Theta(c^k)$ for some fixed $c$. Since $7^3 y^2$ is powerful, for such an $x_k$ one can verify that the triple $$(x_k-2)^2, (x_k-1)^2, x_k^2-2$$is a three-term arithmetic progression of powerful integers. As proven by Narumi and Tachiya (see their Corollary $1$), the set of $x \in \mathbb{N}$ for which the half-open interval $[(x-2)^2, x^2)$ contains exactly three powerful numbers has positive density. In particular, if this happens for $x = x_k$, then we have a three-term arithmetic progression of consecutive powerful integers. I therefore conjecture that there are actually $\gg \log n$ consecutive terms $n_k, n_{k+1}, n_{k+2}$ below $n$ that are in arithmetic progression.
